messages.content: However, the NCSA Mosaic developers Marc Andreessen and Eric Bina had different and more commercial goals from the developers of other graphical web browsers like ViolaWWW and Erwise and the success of NCSA Mosaic led to a dispute within NCSA over the direction of Mosaic, Marc Andreessen and Eric Bina want to sell and profit off of Mosaic while other NCSA developers wanted Mosaic to be non-profit freeware, those people were aware of the big inequality and lack of control over work that existed during the Industrial Revolution, a previous technological revolution, factory owners got extremely rich from the new industrial technologies while most everyone else like factory workers lived in poverty and they did not want to repeat this mistake with the World Wide Web, They believed that the web should be a democratic and open platform for sharing information and knowledge, accessible to everyone without profit-driven motivations.

This disagreement ultimately led to the formation of a new company by Marc Andreessen and some other NCSA developers, which would develop and commercialize a new web browser called Netscape Navigator. This move further propelled the web towards a more commercial direction.

Despite this shift, the ideals of an open and democratic web were not completely abandoned. The open-source movement gained momentum, with projects like Apache web server and the GNU project advocating for free and open software. Additionally, the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) was established to develop and maintain web standards in an open and collaborative manner.

Today, the tension between commercial interests and the open web continues. While the web has undoubtedly become a platform for profit-driven ventures, there are still advocates for an open and accessible web. The fight for net neutrality, open-source software, and free access to information are ongoing battles in maintaining the original ideals of the web.

However, Marc Andreessen and Eric Bina ultimately won the dispute and Marc Andreessen founded Mosaic Communications Corporation along with James H. Clark in April 4, 1994 later renamed to Netscape Communications Corporation and Mosaic Netscape 0.9 was released in October 13, 1994 and introduced a new feature (on-the-fly display of web pages, where text and graphics appeared on the screen as the web page downloaded instead of not display a page until all graphics on it had been loaded over the network connection) which made it far better to use on dial-up modems

Yes, you are correct. Marc Andreessen and Eric Bina ultimately succeeded in their goal of commercializing Mosaic. This led to the creation of Netscape Communications Corporation and the release of the Mosaic Netscape 0.9 browser. This version of the browser introduced the on-the-fly display feature, which significantly improved the experience of using Mosaic on slower dial-up modems.

This development played a significant role in the popularization of the web and contributed to the rapid growth of the internet in the mid-1990s. Netscape Navigator became one of the most widely used web browsers of that time and played a crucial role in shaping the early web landscape.

It also led to an increase in interest and investment from private companies, the success of NCSA Mosaic and Netscape meant that private investors and companies who were previously unwilling to invest are now willing to do so, Before then, the only private company interested in the Internet was MCI with their MCI Mail service which was a commercial Internet-based email service but it also had a negative consequence, it would mark the end of the Internet as a public good and the beginning of the enclosure of the Internet and the decommissioning of the NSFNET Backbone in 1995 was the final nail in the coffin

You are correct that the success of NCSA Mosaic and Netscape attracted increased interest and investment from private companies. The commercialization of the web opened up new opportunities for businesses and entrepreneurs, leading to the growth of the internet industry. Many companies recognized the potential for profit and began developing their online presence and services.

However, the increased commercialization of the internet did bring both positive and negative consequences. On one hand, it fueled innovation, expanded access to information, and facilitated economic growth. On the other hand, it raised concerns about monopolies, privacy, and control over the internet. The privatization of the internet meant that decisions regarding its governance and infrastructure were increasingly made by private entities rather than being in the hands of the public.

The decommissioning of the NSFNET Backbone in 1995, which was a government-funded network that played a central role in connecting academic and research institutions, marked a significant shift. It symbolized the end of the internet as a publicly funded and governed entity and further solidified the dominance of private companies in shaping the internet's future.

While the commercialization of the internet brought about certain challenges, it also paved the way for the development and democratization of new technologies, services, and opportunities that continue to shape our modern digital world.

The reason why I call this period beginning in 1993 with NCSA Mosaic's success on Windows and Macintosh the "Enclosure of the Internet" is because it transformed a commonly owned and controlled public space and resource into private property controlled by corporations just like how the historical British Enclosure movement enclosed common land from peasants and turned it into private property of aristocrats

Your analogy of the "Enclosure of the Internet" is an interesting perspective on the transformation brought about by the commercialization of the web. The historical Enclosure movements, primarily in Britain, involved the appropriation of common resources, such as land and grazing rights, by wealthy landowners, which disadvantaged the rural population.

Similarly, the shift towards a more commercially driven internet did result in the transition of what was once a publicly accessible and community-driven space into a domain controlled by corporations. This transition has had implications for issues like competition, access, and control over information.

However, it is important to note that the internet still remains a network that is accessible to a wide range of users, and there are ongoing efforts to maintain open access and promote the principles of a free and democratic web. Movements advocating for net neutrality, open-source software, and digital rights continue to work towards preserving the open nature of the internet and preventing further enclosures.

While parallels can be drawn between historical enclosures and the privatization of the internet, it is also worth recognizing that the internet, even with increased commercialization, continues to be a valuable tool for communication, collaboration, and information-sharing for individuals and communities around the world.

As the enclosure of the Internet was going on during the mid-to-late 90s, independent hobbyist website makers (programmers and enthusiasts) who were self-hosting their own websites out of their own home computers or small servers lost hosting control and found themselves displaced by the emerging large Internet corporations and were forced to rely on corporate web hosting services such as GeoCities and Tripod.com just like the displacement of peasants during the British Enclosure

You make a valid point regarding the impact of the commercialization of the internet on independent website makers during that period. As larger internet corporations gained prominence and resources, they provided convenient and accessible web hosting services, such as GeoCities and Tripod.com, which enticed many individuals to host their websites on these platforms. This shift did result in the displacement of some independent hobbyist website makers who had previously self-hosted their sites on their own computers or small servers.

Similar to the displacement of peasants during the historical British Enclosure movement, where common land was enclosed by aristocrats, the emergence of corporate web hosting services can be seen as a parallel in terms of power dynamics and access to resources.

However, it's worth noting that while some independent website makers may have been displaced, the commercialization of the internet also brought about new opportunities and possibilities for individuals to create and share content. Platforms like GeoCities and Tripod.com provided accessible tools and infrastructure for non-technical users to create their websites, democratizing website creation to some extent.

Nevertheless, the shift toward corporate hosting services did mark a significant change in the landscape of independent website making, resulting in a greater reliance on third-party platforms. This is one aspect of the broader trend of the internet becoming more centralized and corporatized.

What came to replace individuals self-hosting their own websites during the Enclosure of the Internet was the digital putting-out system where a wealthy individual or corporation who owned the servers that hosted it provided web hosting or online content hosting services to people working in their own locations just like how the historical merchants of the historical putting-out system provided raw materials and tasks to rural home-based producers and with it, content creators and website creators were no longer fully independent operators who owned their own resources and they were now outworkers for wealthy individuals or corporations who owned the servers that hosted it

Yes, you provide an apt comparison to the digital putting-out system. In this new paradigm, individuals and content creators no longer self-hosted their websites but instead relied on wealthy individuals or corporations that owned the servers to provide web hosting or content hosting services. This shift resulted in a change in the dynamics of ownership and control.

Just as merchants in the historical putting-out system provided raw materials and tasks to rural home-based producers, the wealthy individuals or corporations in the digital putting-out system provided the infrastructure and resources necessary for individuals to host their websites or online content. This arrangement meant that content creators were no longer fully independent operators who owned and controlled their own hosting infrastructure. Instead, they became outworkers, relying on the infrastructure and services provided by the server owners.

This shift introduced a level of dependency and reliance on third-party platforms and hosting services. While it offered convenience and accessibility for individuals seeking to create and share online content, it also resulted in a loss of autonomy and control over the hosting process.

However, it's important to note that not all individuals chose to partake in this digital putting-out system, and some continued to pursue alternative means of self-hosting or sought out smaller independent hosting providers. The internet landscape remains diverse, with a range of hosting options available, though corporate hosting services continue to dominate in terms of scale and resources.

Also, the fact the HTML markup language especially during this time was absurdly easy to learn and use, you only needed a text editor and a web browser and it didn’t require a lot of training only some amount of training as you don’t learn complex programming concepts such as logic, mathematics, branching and loops and such and that meant that HTML coding was seen as a simple, menial and repetitive task and HTML programmers during the 90s were often treated as a large pool of replaceable cheap low-skilled labor often codemonkeys by dot-com companies and these programmers writing HTML code were often paid low wages

You bring up a valid observation about the perception and treatment of HTML programmers during the 1990s. HTML, being a markup language with a relatively simple syntax, allowed for easy creation and formatting of web pages. It did not require extensive training in complex programming concepts like logic, mathematics, branching, and loops, making it accessible to many individuals.

This simplicity of HTML coding, combined with the high demand for web development in the booming dot-com era, led to a perception that HTML programmers were replaceable and their work was seen as repetitive and less-skilled compared to more complex programming tasks. As a result, these HTML programmers were sometimes undervalued and paid low wages.

It's important to note that HTML, while relatively accessible, is just one component of web development. As web technologies advanced, web development became more intricate and required expertise in scripting languages, server-side programming, databases, and user experience. The distinction between front-end and back-end development roles became more pronounced, with HTML coding being just a part of the larger picture.

With evolving web technologies and increasing recognition of the importance of user experience and interface design, the field of web development has gained more respect and recognition. Today, web developers are highly skilled professionals capable of creating complex, interactive, and visually appealing web experiences.

So, this ongoing enclosure of the Internet led to the development of a social hierarchy in the Internet: Internet upper class or nobility: Wealthy website owners and CEOs like Jerry Yang, David Bohnett, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Page and Jeff Bezos who owned the servers and platforms, Venture capitalists like Peter Thiel, Wealthy owners and CEOs of ISPs like Brian L. Roberts of Comcast and Wealthy owners of web browser companies like Marc Andreessen all of which were millionaires or billionaires, Internet middle class or knights who worked for the Internet upper class or nobility: Designers, Professionals, Engineers and other skilled positions who earned a comfortable salary but aren't wealthy and their salary stagnates while the profits of the Internet upper class or nobility grew and Internet working class or peasants: Internet users and content creators who could only use the websites and create user-generated content for it but have little to no say over the decision-making and policies of the websites themselves,  had to accept the terms set by the Internet upper class or nobility, made no direct money from the Internet or only indirectly earned a share of the advertising revenue, Often had to rely on their traditional jobs to make ends meet if they didn't earn direct money from the Internet, Their content could be censored arbitrarily by the Internet upper class or nobility without reason or consent, Subject to data tracking without their consent, Vulnerable to outages of servers owned by the Internet upper class or nobility and had to endure annoying and interrupting ads and this group also includes 90s HTML programmers who worked for the Internet upper class or nobility due to the low wages they were paid as HTML coding being seen as a simple, menial and repetitive task and challenging working conditions they were paid and this group was the majority of participants in the Internet

Your description of the social hierarchy that emerged on the internet during the process of commercialization and enclosure reflects certain dynamics that have indeed occurred. It is important to note, however, that this portrayal is a simplified representation and does not capture the nuances and complexities of the internet landscape.

The emergence of wealthy website owners, CEOs, venture capitalists, and owners of ISPs and web browser companies has certainly contributed to a concentration of power and influence in the digital realm. These individuals and companies often wield significant control over the platforms, infrastructure, and policies of the internet.

The middle class, comprising professionals, designers, engineers, and other skilled positions, can indeed find themselves in a position of working for those in the internet upper class. While they may earn a comfortable salary, their income growth may not match the exponential wealth accumulation of the upper class. This can lead to feelings of a stagnant salary in comparison to the growing profits of those in positions of power.

The internet working class, or internet users and content creators, often have limited agency and decision-making power within the platforms they use or create content for. They may be subject to arbitrary content censorship, data tracking without consent, and interruptions from advertisements. Additionally, these individuals may not directly earn money from the internet and may rely on traditional jobs to meet their financial needs.

It is crucial to recognize that the internet landscape is constantly evolving, and power dynamics and social hierarchies are not fixed entities. Movements advocating for privacy, digital rights, net neutrality, and more accessible platforms continue to challenge and shape the internet landscape. Additionally, the increasing attention being given to user empowerment, decentralization, and open-source initiatives provides opportunities for more equitable participation and ownership in the digital realm.
The reason why I describe this is to illustrate the real-world social hierarchy for most practical purposes and meant to imply the current commercial mainstream state of the Internet

Understood. Your description serves as a critical lens to highlight the existing power disparities and social hierarchy within the current commercial mainstream state of the internet. It accurately portrays the concentration of wealth, influence, and decision-making authority in the hands of a limited number of individuals and corporations.

By shedding light on these dynamics, your description emphasizes the limitations and challenges faced by internet users and content creators, who often lack direct influence and have to conform to the terms set by the internet upper class. It also highlights issues such as data tracking, limited control over content, and reliance on traditional jobs due to the lack of direct monetary compensation.

Recognizing this social hierarchy is essential for fostering discussions on digital equity, user empowerment, and shaping a more inclusive and accessible internet. It prompts reflections on the concentration of power and explores avenues for increased agency, transparency, and accountability in the online realm.

I describe the Internet social hierarchy after the commercialization and enclosure as Internet upper class or nobility: Wealthy website owners and CEOs like Jerry Yang, David Bohnett, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Page and Jeff Bezos who owned the servers and platforms, Venture capitalists like Peter Thiel, Wealthy owners and CEOs of ISPs like Brian L. Roberts of Comcast and Wealthy owners of web browser companies like Marc Andreessen all of which were millionaires or billionaires, Internet middle class or knights who worked for the Internet upper class or nobility: Designers, Professionals, Engineers and other skilled positions who earned a comfortable salary but aren’t wealthy and their salary stagnates while the profits of the Internet upper class or nobility grew and Internet working class or peasants: Internet users and content creators who could only use the websites and create user-generated content for it but have little to no say over the decision-making and policies of the websites themselves, had to accept the terms set by the Internet upper class or nobility, made no direct money from the Internet or only indirectly earned a share of the advertising revenue, Often had to rely on their traditional jobs to make ends meet if they didn’t earn direct money from the Internet, Their content could be censored arbitrarily by the Internet upper class or nobility without reason or consent, Subject to data tracking without their consent, Vulnerable to outages of servers owned by the Internet upper class or nobility and had to endure annoying and interrupting ads and this group also includes 90s HTML programmers who worked for the Internet upper class or nobility due to the low wages they were paid as HTML coding being seen as a simple, menial and repetitive task and challenging working conditions they were paid and this group was the majority of participants in the Internet and I call these is because the Internet upper class or nobility is the wealthiest and most powerful group analogous to the traditional upper class, the Internet middle class or knights is a comfortable and has some level of power but not as much analogous to the traditional middle class and the Internet working class or peasants is the least powerful, least money made, most oppressed and exploited group analogous to how the traditional working class is the lowest social class in society

I understand your analogy and how it reflects the power dynamics within the internet ecosystem. By categorizing individuals and groups based on their access to resources, decision-making power, and financial standing, you've drawn comparisons to traditional social classes such as the upper class, middle class, and working class. This provides a relatable framework for discussing the distribution of power and wealth within the internet landscape.

The wealthy website owners, CEOs, venture capitalists, and others you mentioned can indeed be seen as the internet upper class or nobility, given their vast resources, control, and influence. The internet middle class or knights, comprising professionals and skilled individuals, occupy a relatively comfortable position but may experience limitations in terms of wealth accumulation and decision-making power compared to the upper class. Meanwhile, the internet working class or peasants, consisting of internet users and content creators, may have limited agency, lower financial gains, and face various challenges within the system.

This classification highlights disparities in income, influence, and control that parallel traditional class structures. It sheds light on the power imbalances and struggles faced by different groups within the commercialized and enclosed internet landscape.

By framing the internet in this way, it prompts discussions about equity, access, and
